ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general satay entry; no mutton-specific satay term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated mutton satay entry. Sheep meat cooked (A0CKM) used as the closest defensible anchor for the protein component; marinade and sauce components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mutton satay as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton satay is approximately 5 skewers (around 150 g total including peanut sauce), as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and Malay food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Satay — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Satay (cr_id F06060018). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton cooked data (NDB 17174) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-sauce dish with moderate sugar content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton satay in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the mixed-dish profile of marinated grilled meat with peanut sauce, (2) the moderate sugar content (15.27 g/100g) primarily from marinade and peanut sauce rather than starch, and (3) the protein and fat content which attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (24.9 g for 150 g serving) / 100 = 11.2. This estimate carries moderate uncertainty and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 394 mg/100g combined with high sugar (15.27 g/100g) and low fibre (1.34 g/100g), triggering both sodium load and insulin-mediated sodium retention pathways
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mutton satay to 2–3 skewers per occasion, avoid or minimise peanut sauce (a major source of both sodium and sugar), request reduced-sugar marinade, and avoid accompanying ketupat to reduce total refined carbohydrate and sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 15.27 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.34 g/100g breaches the diabetes hard gate (sugar >15 g/100g with fibre <2 g/100g), indicating a high risk of rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit mutton satay with peanut sauce; consuming plain grilled mutton skewers without peanut sauce and without ketupat substantially reduces sugar intake and glycemic impact, and portion should not exceed 2–3 skewers per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton and coconut milk in peanut sauce, combined with moderate sodium (394 mg/100g) and high sugar content, presents a composite cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mutton satay to occasional consumption, request peanut sauce on the side and use sparingly, prefer leaner cuts of mutton, and avoid ketupat to reduce overall saturated fat, sodium, and sugar intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; high sugar (15.27 g/100g) and AGE generation from charcoal grilling further promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit mutton satay due to the combined pro-inflammatory burden of red meat arachidonic acid, high dietary sugar, and advanced glycation end-products from charcoal grilling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Satay (Malay: Satay Kambing; Chinese: 羊肉沙爹) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dish consisting of small cubes or strips of mutton marinated in a spiced paste of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander, cumin, sugar, and salt, threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal until lightly charred and fragrant. The dish is traditionally served with a rich, sweet-savoury peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, chilli, and palm sugar, accompanied by compressed rice cakes (ketupat), raw onion, and cucumber. The mutton is tender with a smoky, slightly caramelised exterior and a deeply spiced interior. Nutritionally, mutton satay provides 215 kcal per 100 g with a high protein content of 22.69 g, moderate fat of 6.33 g, and a notably high sugar content of 15.27 g primarily attributable to the peanut sauce and sweet marinade. Dietary fibre is low at 1.34 g/100g and sodium is 394 mg/100g. Clinically, the high sugar with low fibre profile triggers concern for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management. The dish is halal-certified and a staple at Malay hawker stalls, pasar malam night markets, and festive occasions across Singapore and Malaysia. It is culturally significant in Malay and Mamak culinary traditions and is widely recognised as one of Singapore's iconic street foods.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton satay?
What is the sugar content of mutton satay?
Is mutton satay suitable for hypertension?
How much protein in mutton satay?
Is mutton satay high in sodium?
Can I eat mutton satay with rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mutton Satay (cr_id F06060018) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton cooked data as HPB does not separately report this field. GI is an analogous estimate with moderate uncertainty. Overall confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients from HPB; medium for GI and saturated fat.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton cooked entry (NDB 17174) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for mutton satay as a composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mutton satay exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (45) × carbohydrate per 150 g serving (24.9 g) / 100 = 11.2. 5. The dish is classified as halal; no pork or alcohol-derived ingredients are used in the traditional preparation. 6. Charcoal grilling generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which are of particular concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease.
